name: Complex Numbers — Meeting i = √(−1)
whatIsIt: Some equations, like x² = −1, have no real answer because no real number squares to a negative. Mathematicians invented the IMAGINARY UNIT i with i² = −1. A COMPLEX NUMBER is a + bi, where a is the real part and b the imaginary part. Its MODULUS (size) is |a + bi| = √(a² + b²). Powers of i cycle: i, −1, −i, 1.
whyNeeded: Complex numbers solve every polynomial equation and are essential in electrical engineering (AC circuits), signal processing, physics and computer graphics. They also make rotations and waves easy to describe.
realLifeUses: Describing rotations and waves in physics

vocabulary.meaning: The number with i² = −1, so i = √(−1).
A number of the form a + bi, with real part a and imaginary part b.
Its distance from 0 on the plane: √(a² + b²).
They cycle every four: i¹ = i, i² = −1, i³ = −i, i⁴ = 1.

story.title: The Impossible Square Root
story.text: Dev is stuck: x² = −1 has no real answer, since a square is never negative. Sir smiles: 'Mathematicians invented a new number, i, with i² = −1.' Now x = i works. Sir plots 3 + 4i on a grid and asks its size. Dev computes the modulus: √(3² + 4²) = √(9 + 16) = √25 = 5. 'A brand-new number system, and it's genuinely useful,' says Sir.
story.extractedProblem: Find the modulus of the complex number 3 + 4i.

shapes.label: 3 + 4i plotted on the Argand plane; distance from 0 is 5

visual.caption: A complex number a + bi is a point (a, b) on the plane; its modulus is its distance √(a² + b²) from the origin.

teachingGallery.title: i² = −1 drives everything
teachingGallery.note: Replace i² by −1 wherever it appears; modulus is a Pythagoras distance.

props.label: i² = −1 sits off the real line

examples.caption: No real number squares to −1, so i lives off the real number line.
In 3 + 4i the real part is 3 and the imaginary part is 4.

standardMethod.summary: Treat i like a variable but replace i² by −1. Add/subtract by matching real and imaginary parts. Modulus = √(real² + imaginary²).
steps: Write each number as a + bi (real part a, imaginary part b).
Add or subtract by combining real parts together and imaginary parts together.
Multiply like binomials, then replace every i² with −1.
For powers of i, reduce using the cycle i, −1, −i, 1 (repeats every 4).

commonMistakes.fix: By definition i² = −1. That negative sign is the whole point of complex numbers.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding a real part to an imaginary part (e.g. 2 + 3i = 5).
commonMistakes.fix: Real and imaginary parts stay separate: 2 + 3i cannot be simplified to a single real number.

easy.q: In the complex number 7 + 2i, what is the real part?
hintLadder: Real part is the plain number.

challenge.q: A student writes i² = 1. What's the fix?

questions.q: A complex number has the form…

options.label: a + bi, with real part a and imaginary part b

questions.answer: a + bi, with real part a and imaginary part b

teachBackPrompt: Explain to a grown-up why x² = −1 needs a new number i with i² = −1, plot 3 + 4i as a point, and show its modulus is √(3² + 4²) = 5 — a Pythagoras distance.

revisionCard.summary: i = √(−1), so i² = −1. Complex number = a + bi (real a, imaginary b). Add by matching parts; multiply and replace i² by −1. Modulus |a + bi| = √(a² + b²). Powers of i cycle every 4: i, −1, −i, 1.

rememberIt.unpack: z = a + bi. Modulus |z| = sqrt(a^2 + b^2). Powers of i cycle every 4: i, -1, -i, 1. Conjugate of a+bi is a-bi.

studentQuestions.q: How can sqrt(-1) be a number?
studentQuestions.a: We DEFINE a new number i whose square is -1. It obeys the usual algebra rules and lets us solve equations that reals cannot.
Powers of i repeat every 4 (i, -1, -i, 1). 100 divided by 4 leaves remainder 0, so i^100 = 1.

formulas.name: Powers of i
formulas.remember: Cycle of 4 - use the remainder of the power / 4
